flashcards.back: The property that a globally optimal solution can be achieved by making a series of locally optimal choices.
The property that an optimal solution to a problem contains optimal solutions to its subproblems.

flashcards.front: Name three classic problems solved by greedy algorithms.
flashcards.back: Activity selection, Huffman coding, and fractional knapsack problems.

flashcards.front: Why doesn't the greedy approach work for the 0-1 knapsack problem?
flashcards.back: Because taking items based on value-to-weight ratio doesn't guarantee optimal solution due to the all-or-nothing nature of item selection.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of greedy algorithms over dynamic programming?
flashcards.back: Greedy algorithms are typically more efficient and have lower memory requirements.
